metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = extended target / form factor suppressed / structure visible; CRIMSON = point target / no suppression / no structure
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no form-factor integral computation, no Born-amplitude derivation, no deep-inelastic extension

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Fire a beam of electrons at a nucleus. Count how many bounce off at each angle. The pattern of bright and dark rings in the count looks like the diffraction pattern from a slit — except here, the slit is the nucleus itself.

props.headline: Fire a beam of electrons at a nucleus. Count how many…

beats.narration_text: The first place where the count hits zero — the first dark ring — sits at an angle that depends on nothing but the size of the nucleus. Read that angle. Get the radius. This is how the proton's size was first measured.

props.headline: The first place where the count hits zero — the first…

beats.narration_text: A diffraction pattern has a dark ring. The dark ring's angle encodes the size of the object that made it. How does a scattering experiment off a nucleus become a diffraction experiment that measures nuclear size?

props.headline: A diffraction pattern has a dark ring. The dark ring's angle…

card.headline: The first dark ring measures the nucleus.
card.dek: How does the angle of a zero in scattering encode the radius of a target you cannot see?

beats.narration_text: A point target scatters electrons in all directions equally — every angle gets the same count, no rings, no zeros. An extended target is different. Electrons scattered from opposite edges of the target can interfere. Constructive interference makes bright spots. Destructive interference makes dark ones.

props.headline: A point target scatters electrons in all directions equally — every…

beats.narration_text: The full scattering pattern from an extended target is the product of two things: the pattern you would get from a point — which falls off with angle — times a form factor that carries the interference from the target's shape. The form factor starts at one and drops as you go to larger angles.

props.headline: The full scattering pattern from an extended target is the product…

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The full scattering pattern from an extended target is the product of two things: the pattern you would get from a point — which falls off with angle — times a

beats.narration_text: The form factor depends on the momentum transfer — the kick the electron gives the nucleus at a given angle. Small angle means small kick, long-range probing, form factor near one. Large angle means large kick, short-range probing, form factor small.

props.headline: The form factor depends on the momentum transfer — the kick…

beats.narration_text: The form factor is the Fourier transform of the charge distribution. For a sphere, it oscillates as the kick grows — constructive at some transfers, destructive at others. The first zero occurs when the momentum-transfer times the radius hits a specific value: about 4.49.

props.headline: The form factor is the Fourier transform of the charge distribution.…

beats.narration_text: At that first zero, electrons scattered from the left half of the nucleus and electrons scattered from the right half are exactly out of step — perfect destructive interference. The count drops to zero. The angle where this happens is the dark ring.

props.headline: At that first zero, electrons scattered from the left half of…

beats.narration_text: The momentum transfer at scattering angle theta is two times the beam momentum times the sine of half the angle. So if the first dark ring appears at angle theta-zero, the nuclear radius is 4.49 divided by the momentum transfer at that angle. One angle reading, one radius.

props.headline: The momentum transfer at scattering angle theta is two times the…

beats.narration_text: Larger nucleus — first zero at smaller angle. Smaller nucleus — first zero pushed to larger angle. The dark ring moves inward as the target gets bigger, outward as it shrinks. The size is written in the angle.

props.headline: Larger nucleus — first zero at smaller angle. Smaller nucleus —…

card.headline: First zero at q times R = 4.49. Larger R: smaller angle. Smaller R: larger angle.
card.dek: The form factor is the Fourier transform of the charge distribution. Its first zero encodes the radius.

beats.narration_text: Robert Hofstadter did this experiment in 1956 with an electron accelerator at Stanford. He fired electrons at hydrogen targets and found the first dark ring in the proton's form factor. The proton's charge radius came out to roughly 0.85 femtometers — less than a trillionth of a millimeter.

props.headline: Robert Hofstadter did this experiment in 1956 with an electron accelerator…

beats.narration_text: The same method works for every nucleus. Heavy nuclei are larger — the first dark ring appears at smaller angles. The nuclear radius follows a simple scaling: proportional to the cube root of the mass number. Every nucleus measured, from helium to uranium, obeys the same pattern.

props.headline: The same method works for every nucleus. Heavy nuclei are larger…

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: fire electrons with momentum 250 MeV per c at a nucleus. Momentum in inverse femtometers: 250 divided by 197 equals about 1.27 inverse femtometers. The first dark ring appears at 20 degrees. Momentum transfer: 2 times 1.27 times sine of 10 degrees, equals 0.44 inverse femtometers. Radius: 4.49 divided by 0.44, about 10 femtometers. That is consistent with a heavy nucleus with mass number around 200. One dark ring, one radius.

props.headline: Illustrative: fire electrons with momentum 250 MeV per c at a…

beats.narration_text: The question: how does a scattering experiment become a measurement of nuclear size? The answer: an extended target is an interferometer. Electrons scattered from different parts interfere, and the form factor — the Fourier transform of the charge distribution — encodes that interference. Its first dark ring tells you where the momentum transfer times the radius equals 4.49. One angle. One number. Quantum mechanics.

props.headline: The question: how does a scattering experiment become a measurement of…

card.headline: First dark ring at q times R = 4.49. Read the angle, get the radius.
card.dek: The extended target is its own interferometer. The form factor is its Fourier transform.
